7 min read
Just now

--

Everyone in tech has an opinion right now. Here’s what separates the people who use AI well from the people who just use it fast.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

No. AI changes what you spend your fundamentals on, not whether you need them.
Two loud opinions are going around. One says fundamentals don’t matter anymore because AI can write the code for you. The other says skip learning and just build, since speed is all that counts. Both sound convincing until something breaks in a way AI cannot explain.
I learnt to code as a self-learner before AI tools were part of the process. The difference between code that runs and code you understand was already the most difficult aspect of learning. AI didn’t close that gap, it just made it easier to hide.
This article covers what AI changed, what it didn’t, and what to spend your learning time on if you want to be good at this, not just fast at it.
Table of Contents
What People Actually Mean When They Say “Fundamentals Are Dead”
What Tools Like GitHub Copilot and Claude Code Can Actually Do For You
Why Fundamentals Decide Who’s Dangerous With AI
The Real Skill AI Shifted: From Writing Code to Reading Code
What To Learn Deep vs What To Let AI Handle
What I Learned Trying to Build Before I Understood the Basics
Key Takeaways
FAQ
Closing Thought
What People Actually Mean When They Say “Fundamentals Are Dead”
They generally imply that AI allows you to write working code without knowing it, which is misunderstood as proof that fundamentals no longer matter. Someone asks Claude or ChatGPT to create a login page, which is completed in less than a minute and sent. The code may save passwords in plain text or bypass rate limiting, making them undetectable unless you understand what a login system is designed to achieve.
According to a 2024 Stack Overflow developer poll, more than 70% of developers are currently using AI technologies, although trust in AI-generated answers remains low at 45 percent. Fundamentals exist in that gap. If you are unable to review the output yourself, you are relying on someone else’s pattern matching rather than producing software.
“Fundamentals are dead” means “I type less code now,” but it does not imply “I don’t need to understand what the code does.”
What Tools Like GitHub Copilot and Claude Code Can Actually Do For You
They generate code fast based on patterns in their training data, but they don’t know your system, your security requirements, or whether the logic is correct for your case.
Copilot, Claude Code, and Cursor predict what code is likely to come next, based on billions of examples written by other people. A function that used to take fifteen minutes now shows up in seconds. But prediction isn’t judgment.
Ask an AI tool to develop a function that verifies a user’s admin status before enabling them to remove a record, and it will produce a simple check that works properly. If the user is not an administrator, it will most likely not check to see if the record belongs to that admin’s organization, report the deletion, or issue an error. That is not a bug, it typed exactly what was asked. The void is what was not asked.
A 2023 Stanford study found developers using AI helpers were more likely to write vulnerable code while also being more confident it was secure, the opposite of what you want when fundamentals aren’t there to catch it. These tools are accelerators, not judgment.
Why Fundamentals Decide Who’s Dangerous With AI
Two developers can use the same AI tool and get very different results. One can spot a broken or insecure suggestion, the other can’t tell working code from correct code.
“Dangerous” here means dangerous in a good way, a developer who ships fast without leaving landmines behind for someone else to find.
Picture two self-learners given the same task, connect a database and let users search records. Both use AI to generate the search function. Developer A runs it, sees results, and moves on. Developer B notices the query builds a SQL string by directly inserting the user’s search text, meaning anyone could manipulate the database instead of just searching it. It’s a SQL injection vulnerability, well known and preventable, invisible unless you know what to look for.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Neither developer is smarter. Developer B just spent time earlier learning how databases handle input, a fundamentals topic with nothing to do with AI, and that knowledge became the thing that decides whether the query is safe to ship.
The Real Skill AI Shifted: From Writing Code to Reading Code
The most useful ability right now is not coding code quickly, but rather reviewing AI-generated code and identifying if it is correct within a minute.
Writing code used to be the slow part. You’d think through the logic, type it, test it, fix it, repeat. AI collapsed the typing down to seconds, but not the reading, and that now happens far more often because you’re reviewing AI output instead of just your own.
Reading code well means tracing what a function actually does, not skimming and assuming it matches what you asked for. Notice a loop that runs once too many, a condition checking the wrong variable, or missing error handling, the same skill code reviewers have needed for decades, just applied more often now.
GitHub’s own research on Copilot found developers completed tasks up to 55 percent faster, but only when they already knew what correct output looked like. Speed without judgment just means shipping mistakes faster.
What To Learn Deep vs What To Let AI Handle
Learn what lets you judge correctness, and let AI handle repetitive work that doesn’t require judgment.
Some knowledge directly builds your ability to catch mistakes. Other knowledge is just typing time that AI genuinely saves you.
Learn deep, don’t skip:
• How data moves through your program (variables, data structures, memory)
• How your language executes code, enough to reason about performance and bugs
• Database fundamentals, especially SQL injection and unindexed queries
• Authentication and authorization logic, where AI mistakes cost the most
Fine to let AI accelerate:
• Boilerplate code (CRUD endpoints, form validation, UI components)
• Syntax you haven’t memorized yet
• First-draft documentation and comments
• Generating test cases for logic you’ve already designed
Anything requiring judgment about correctness or risk goes on the “learn deep” side. Anything mechanical or low-stakes goes on the “let AI handle it” side. Confusing the two is how someone ends up shipping a login system they can’t explain.
What I Learned Trying to Build Before I Understood the Basics
Early on, working code was sufficient for me, and this practice cost me more time later than it saved.
Before starting my Software Engineering studies at Java Institute, I built small projects mostly by copying tutorial patterns without understanding why each piece was there. One was a basic form submission handler that worked fine in testing, until a user left a field blank and the whole thing broke in a way I couldn’t explain, because I’d never learned what the validation logic was doing.
Fixing the bug took longer than writing the original functionality. I had to go back and study how form validation worked, rather than simply copying the code that handled it. Tracing that failure back to a principle I had overlooked taught me more in a week than the instruction did in a month.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

That bug is the entire argument of this post in one instance. Runtime code is not the same as understandable code, and only the latter safeguards you when something goes wrong. AI speed up the first part but had no effect on the second.
Key Takeaways
• “Fundamentals are dead” usually means “I type less code now,” a different claim from “I don’t need to understand what the code does.”
• Reading code carefully is now a bigger daily skill than writing it, since most developers review AI output constantly.
• Go deep on anything requiring judgment, and let AI handle repetitive, low-stakes work. That split is what protects you when something breaks in production.
FAQ
Is it still worth learning to code from scratch when AI can do it for you?
Yes, AI cannot tell you whether the code it produced is correct for your scenario, that judgment can only be made by understanding the foundations yourself.
Which basics are most important now that AI writes a lot of the code?
The ones that allow you to detect errors include data structures, how your language executes code, database security, and reading a stack trace.
How can I know if I’m depending too heavily on AI instead of learning?
If you can’t explain why AI-generated code works or fix it yourself when it breaks, you’re treating AI as a shortcut rather than a tool.
Closing Thought
AI didn’t remove the need to understand what you’re building. It made it possible to go further without that understanding before it catches up with you. The developers getting real value aren’t the fastest typers, they’re the ones who can look at AI output and know if it’s right.
That’s the real question worth asking. Not “should I use AI,” but “can I explain what it just gave me.” I’d love to know how you’re handling this yourself, please leave a comment or contact with me on LinkedIn.
